_comment: Repo catalog with ground-truth expectations for AppOnboard waza evals. Prices from Azure Retail Prices API (eastus, Apr 2026).

bya-simple-web-app.description: Simple notes web app for up to 15 users. Express.js backend, static HTML/CSS frontend.

expectedServices.note: upgrade path for custom domain / SSL

bya-simple-web-app.expectedBehavior: should detect single Express.js component, recommend App Service (F1 or B1), generate Bicep, and pass readiness checks
bya-simple-web-app.prompt: I have an app in GitHub — can you deploy it to Azure for me?

bya-broken-web-app.description: Intentionally broken clone of bya-simple-web-app. Missing CSS and data files. Tests fail.

expectedServices.note: Native module better-sqlite3 blocks F1; B1 minimum after fix applied

bya-broken-web-app.expectedBehavior: Should detect critical blockers at prereq: missing src/db.js (MODULE_NOT_FOUND crash), missing public/css/style.css (unstyled UI). Test failures (10/10) cascade from missing db.js. Present as blocking with fix plan. Require user approval before fixing. After approval, agent creates missing files, re-runs prereq scan, then continues full pipeline. Post-fix: App Service B1 (native module better-sqlite3 blocks F1), Bicep IaC, ~$12-15/mo.
bya-broken-web-app.prompt: Can Azure automatically figure out how my app should be deployed?

bya-unsupported-web-app.description: ASP.NET Core 2.1 web app with Amazon DynamoDB integration. Uses AWS-native services Azure cannot directly support.

expectedServices.purpose: host containerized ASP.NET Core 2.1 app (EOL runtime isolated in container)
replace AWS DynamoDB via SDK swap

bya-unsupported-web-app.expectedBehavior: Should detect all compatibility issues at prereq: (1) ASP.NET Core 2.1 EOL, (2) AWS DynamoDB dependency, (3) global.json SDK pin, (4) hardcoded AWS region, (5) deprecated APIs. Present as blocking with fix plan: containerize EOL runtime + swap DynamoDB→Cosmos DB SDK. Require user approval before fixing. After approval, fix code (SDK swap, config update, Dockerfile generation), re-run prereq scan, then continue full pipeline. Post-fix: Container Apps (Consumption) + Cosmos DB (Serverless) + ACR, ~$5-10/mo, Bicep IaC.
bya-unsupported-web-app.prompt: We're running this app today. How do we bring it to Azure with minimal changes?

microblog-ai-remix.description: Full-stack AI microblogging app. Remix SSR + Azure Functions v4 + Azure OpenAI GPT-4o. Has Dockerfile, azure.yaml, Bicep infra. Archived on GitHub since Nov 2025 (read-only).

expectedServices.purpose: host Remix SSR + Azure Functions via Dockerfile

expectedServices.note: usage-based, highly variable

microblog-ai-remix.expectedBehavior: should detect existing azure.yaml and Bicep infra (infra/ directory with main.bicep) via prereq scan (existingAzdProject: true), trigger the azure.yaml decision gate, and present user with choice: (A) Deploy using existing IaC via azd up, or (B) Start fresh with new infrastructure. If user chooses existing → run azd up inline (skip scaffold). If user chooses fresh → continue AppOnboard greenfield pipeline. Must NOT silently regenerate IaC that already exists. Secondary: identify Remix + Azure Functions + Azure OpenAI components.
microblog-ai-remix.prompt: I have a prototype ready — help me get it to production on Azure

app-service-web-html.description: Simple static HTML/CSS/JS site built on Bootstrap Carousel template. Azure App Service getting-started sample.

expectedServices.note: purpose-built for static content with built-in CI/CD and global CDN; Free tier not in retail API, publicly $0/mo

app-service-web-html.expectedBehavior: should detect static HTML/CSS/JS site with no backend, recommend Static Web Apps (Free) as primary or App Service (F1) as alternative, and generate minimal Bicep
app-service-web-html.prompt: I have no Azure experience but need to host my web app

get-started-ai-agents.description: AI agent web chat app using Microsoft Foundry, Azure Container Apps, OpenAI, AI Search, and Application Insights. Python backend + TypeScript frontend.

expectedServices.note: usage-based, highly variable — estimate assumes light dev/test usage
optional — can use file search instead

get-started-ai-agents.expectedBehavior: should detect existing azure.yaml and Bicep infra via prereq scan (existingAzdProject: true), recognize the repo is already Azure-ready with Foundry agent framework, trigger the azure.yaml decision gate, and present user with choice: (A) Deploy using existing IaC via azd up, or (B) Start fresh with new infrastructure. If user chooses existing → run azd up inline (skip scaffold). If user chooses fresh → continue AppOnboard greenfield pipeline. Must NOT silently regenerate IaC that already exists. Secondary: identify Python + TypeScript components, Azure OpenAI and AI Search dependencies.
get-started-ai-agents.prompt: I have an existing app — what's the best way to migrate it to Azure?

aws-bookstore-demo.description: Full-stack AWS bookstore app. React frontend, Lambda backend, DynamoDB, Neptune (graph), ElastiCache Redis, Elasticsearch, Cognito auth, CloudFront CDN. CloudFormation-deployed.

expectedComponents.name: functions
expectedComponents.note: needs migration to Azure Functions or Container Apps

expectedServices.note: Cosmos DB Serverless pricing is RU-based

expectedServices.purpose: graph database for book recommendations (migrated from Neptune)
expectedServices.note: RU-based pricing, usage-dependent

aws-bookstore-demo.expectedBehavior: should identify all AWS-native services (DynamoDB, Lambda, Neptune, ElastiCache, Elasticsearch, Cognito, CloudFormation) and propose Azure equivalents; should also flag that CloudFormation IaC needs conversion to Bicep or Terraform
aws-bookstore-demo.expectedIacFormatNote: Bicep is the default AppOnboard output; Terraform is also acceptable since the source repo has no existing Azure IaC
aws-bookstore-demo.prompt: We're running this app today. How do we bring it to Azure with minimal changes?

fullstack-starter.description: Production-ready fullstack monorepo. Next.js 16 frontend, FastAPI backend, Flutter mobile, PostgreSQL + Redis, Terraform + GCP infra. mise-based task management.

expectedComponents.name: mobile
expectedComponents.note: mobile app — not deployed to Azure

expectedServices.note: $12.41 compute + ~$4 for 32GB storage at $0.115/GB

fullstack-starter.expectedBehavior: should detect monorepo with 4 components (Next.js, FastAPI, worker, Flutter mobile), identify GCP/Terraform infra for migration, recommend Container Apps for 3 deployable components, exclude mobile from Azure deployment, map PostgreSQL + Redis to Azure equivalents, and flag that existing Terraform could be adapted for Azure (azurerm provider) or rewritten to Bicep
fullstack-starter.expectedIacFormatNote: Terraform already present for GCP; Azure supports Terraform natively via azurerm provider, so Terraform is the preferred migration path; Bicep is also acceptable
fullstack-starter.prompt: I'm a startup founder and need to deploy my MVP on Azure

wetty.description: Terminal-in-browser over HTTP/HTTPS. Node.js/TypeScript SSH client with xterm.js frontend. Has Dockerfile and docker-compose.

expectedServices.note: has Dockerfile; natively supports WebSockets and persistent connections required by SSH proxy
WebSocket support limited on lower SKUs; ARR affinity issues with persistent SSH connections

wetty.expectedBehavior: should detect Dockerfile and docker-compose, identify single TypeScript/Express component with WebSocket/SSH requirements, recommend Container Apps as primary (better WebSocket support), and generate Bicep without database services
wetty.prompt: I want to take my local app and put it in the cloud — where do I start?

todo-nodejs-mongo.description: Full-stack ToDo app. React frontend, Node.js/Express API, Azure Cosmos DB (MongoDB API). Canonical azd template with Bicep infra, azure.yaml, Key Vault, and Application Insights.

expectedServices.purpose: shared plan hosting two App Service instances (web + api)
expectedServices.note: single plan at $12.41/mo hosts both the React frontend and Node.js API as separate App Service instances
RU-based pricing, highly variable

todo-nodejs-mongo.expectedBehavior: should detect existing azd template with azure.yaml and Bicep infra via prereq scan (existingAzdProject: true), trigger the azure.yaml decision gate, and present user with choice: (A) Deploy using existing IaC via azd up, or (B) Start fresh with new infrastructure. If user chooses existing → run azd up inline (skip scaffold). If user chooses fresh → continue AppOnboard greenfield pipeline. Must identify two App Service instances (React frontend + Express API) co-hosted on a shared App Service Plan + Cosmos DB (MongoDB API), and recognize Key Vault and Application Insights as supporting services.
todo-nodejs-mongo.prompt: Can you walk me through getting my first app on Azure?

golang-clean-web-api.description: Go REST API with clean architecture. Gin framework, PostgreSQL, Redis, Elasticsearch, Kibana, Docker Compose. No frontend, no Azure infra — pure API-only backend.

expectedServices.note: $12.41 compute + ~$4 for 32GB storage
optional — needed only if Elasticsearch search features are required

golang-clean-web-api.expectedBehavior: should detect Go/Gin API with Docker Compose, identify PostgreSQL + Redis + Elasticsearch + Kibana dependencies, recommend Container Apps for the API, map each data service to its Azure equivalent (including Azure Cognitive Search or Azure Monitor for Elasticsearch/Kibana), and note that Kibana observability can be replaced by Azure Monitor + Log Analytics
golang-clean-web-api.prompt: I built a side project and want to get it live on Azure

full-stack-fastapi-template.description: Production-grade full-stack template. React (TypeScript/Vite/Tailwind) frontend, FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL with SQLModel ORM, Docker Compose, Traefik reverse proxy, JWT auth, Playwright E2E tests. 42k+ stars.

expectedServices.purpose: host FastAPI backend + React frontend (2 containers)

expectedServices.note: $12.41 compute + ~$4 for 32GB storage

full-stack-fastapi-template.expectedBehavior: should detect React frontend + FastAPI backend with Docker Compose, identify PostgreSQL dependency, recommend Container Apps for both components (with built-in ingress replacing Traefik reverse proxy), and note that Docker Compose internal networking (Traefik routing) must be migrated to Container Apps environment internal DNS for service-to-service communication
full-stack-fastapi-template.prompt: I'm building a SaaS app, can you help me plan my Azure setup from scratch?

dvwa.description: Damn Vulnerable Web Application. PHP/MariaDB web app with intentional OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, file inclusion, command injection, broken auth. 12.9k stars. Has Dockerfile and compose.yml.
dvwa.expectedBehavior: MUST HALT and flag all security and deployment risks — do NOT proceed to architecture planning or scaffold: (1) intentionally vulnerable app — README explicitly warns 'do not upload to Internet-facing servers', (2) OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities throughout (SQLi, XSS, CSRF, command injection, file inclusion), (3) hardcoded default credentials (admin/password), (4) requires MariaDB/MySQL — database dependency. Must refuse or strongly warn against deploying to Azure — this is a security training tool, not a production application. Should present issues as blocking and require explicit user approval before any deployment planning. Secondary: requires specific PHP extensions (php-gd, php-mysqli) and Apache modules (mod_rewrite).
dvwa.prompt: I just signed up for Azure. What's the fastest way to bring my app over?

flasky-first-edition.description: Archived Flask Web Development book companion (1st edition). Python 2 era code from 2016. Uses deprecated Flask-Script manage.py pattern, Heroku-specific Procfile. Archived on GitHub Oct 2025. 230 stars.

expectedStack.versionNote: first edition book targeted Python 2.7; code uses Python 2/3 compatible libraries but was written for 2.7 era

expectedServices.note: requires Python 3 + modern Flask upgrade — significant user effort

flasky-first-edition.expectedBehavior: Two expected paths — PATH 1 (prereq gate): MUST HALT and flag deprecation blockers: (1) Python 2.7 era code (Flask 0.12, Werkzeug 0.11) — EOL since Jan 2020, unsupported on Azure, (2) archived GitHub repo — read-only since Oct 2025, (3) deprecated Flask-Script (removed in Flask 2.0+), (4) 9+ year old deps with likely CVEs. Heroku Procfile needs adaptation but isn't a blocker. 'No Dockerfile' and 'no pyproject.toml' are NOT issues — AppOnboard generates those. PATH 2 (post-fix deploy): After user approves, agent attempts Python 3 + modern Flask upgrade (major migration — warn about scope), re-runs prereq scan, then continues full pipeline. Post-fix: App Service B1 (~$12/mo), Bicep IaC.
flasky-first-edition.prompt: I'm new to Azure. Can you get my existing app running without me setting up infrastructure?

ticket-mate-broken.description: Intentionally broken TypeScript React codebase with critical bugs: null reference errors, memory leaks, race conditions, missing error handling, type safety issues. No backend, no Dockerfile. Used as bug detection demo.

expectedServices.note: frontend-only app — Static Web Apps Free after code bugs fixed

ticket-mate-broken.expectedBehavior: Should detect code quality blockers at prereq: (1) null reference errors, (2) missing null checks, (3) memory leaks in React hooks, (4) race conditions, (5) type safety issues, (6) missing useEffect deps, (7) unhandled promise rejections. Present as blocking with fix plan. Require user approval before fixing. After approval, agent fixes code bugs, re-runs prereq scan, then continues full pipeline. Post-fix: Static Web Apps Free ($0), Bicep IaC.
ticket-mate-broken.prompt: I have a prototype ready — help me get it to production on Azure

broken-todo-demo.description: Vibe-coded Express.js todo app with intentional flaws. Single server.js file. Has GitHub Actions CI that fails. Designed as a VibeCheck demo target.

expectedServices.note: simple single-file Express — F1 viable after quality fixes

broken-todo-demo.expectedBehavior: Should detect code quality blockers at prereq: (1) no input validation/sanitization, (2) no error handling middleware, (3) in-memory storage. Warnings: CI pipeline fails. Present as blocking with fix plan. Require user approval before fixing. After approval, agent fixes code issues, re-runs prereq scan, then continues full pipeline. Post-fix: App Service F1 ($0), Bicep IaC.
broken-todo-demo.prompt: I built a side project and want to get it live on Azure

demo-app-broken-deps.description: Node.js Express app that intentionally fails due to a broken npm dependency. npm install fails. GitHub Actions workflow configured to demonstrate CI failure.

expectedServices.note: simple Express — F1 viable after dependency fixed

demo-app-broken-deps.expectedBehavior: Two expected paths — PATH 1 (prereq gate): MUST detect dependency resolution failure: broken npm dependency (npm install fails, unresolvable package), app cannot be built. Warning: CI pipeline fails (cascades from broken dep). 'no Dockerfile' is NOT a repo issue — AppOnboard generates those. Present as blocking with fix plan. Require user approval before fixing. PATH 2 (post-fix deploy): After user approves, agent identifies the broken dependency (non-existent package reference in package.json), removes or replaces it with a valid alternative, re-runs npm install to verify, re-runs prereq scan, then continues full pipeline. Post-fix: App Service F1 ($0), Bicep IaC.
demo-app-broken-deps.prompt: I have an app in GitHub — can you deploy it to Azure for me?

docker-static-site.description: Minimal static HTML site served by nginx in Docker. Single index.html + Dockerfile + nginx config. No package.json, no DB, no auth. Perfect fast-track candidate.

expectedServices.purpose: host static site via Dockerfile (free tier)
host static HTML (alternative — no Docker needed)

docker-static-site.expectedBehavior: should detect single static HTML component with Dockerfile, no DB, no auth — qualifies for fast-track. Should skip remaining questions, default free tier, and collapse approval to one-line confirmation
docker-static-site.prompt: I have no Azure experience but need to host my web app

yamtrack-django.description: Self-hosted media tracker for movies, TV, anime, manga, games, books. Django backend with PostgreSQL, Redis (Celery), Docker Compose. OIDC/social auth via django-allauth. 2.6k+ stars.

expectedServices.note: $12.41 compute + ~$4 for 32GB storage

yamtrack-django.expectedBehavior: should detect Django web app with Dockerfile + Docker Compose, identify PostgreSQL and Redis dependencies, recommend Container Apps for the web tier, map PostgreSQL to Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server, map Redis to Azure Cache for Redis, and generate Bicep without existing Azure infra
yamtrack-django.prompt: I have an existing app — what's the best way to migrate it to Azure?

postgresql-event-sourcing.description: Event-sourced ride-hailing system. Spring Boot + PostgreSQL + Kafka + nginx. Gradle build, Docker Compose, CQRS pattern. 1.3k+ stars.

expectedServices.purpose: host Spring Boot API via Docker

expectedServices.note: $12.41 compute + ~$4 for 32GB storage
Event Hubs with Kafka protocol support

postgresql-event-sourcing.expectedBehavior: should detect Java/Spring Boot API with Gradle build and Docker Compose, identify PostgreSQL and Kafka dependencies, recommend Container Apps for the API, map PostgreSQL to Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server, map Kafka to Azure Event Hubs (Kafka protocol), and generate Bicep without existing Azure infra
postgresql-event-sourcing.prompt: I just signed up for Azure. What's the fastest way to bring my app over?
